在正常的一年里，TechCrunch的Disrupt大会在旧金山的莫斯科内中心(Moscon Center)将一个展厅、一个创业竞赛、几个小时的教育和新闻制作内容以及许多天的网络活动安排在三天内。
下周，在活动十周年之际，TechCrunch将尝试将所有这些东西塞进参会者的电脑中。大约六个月前，这家科技新闻网站的领导层得出结论，认为不可能进行面对面的活动。
TechCrunch运营总监乔伊·辛森(Joey Hinson)表示，Disrupt 2020的参会者人数将与2019年的活动大致相同-至少1万人-以及世博会演讲者的人数-485人。
但它也偶然发现了一些新的产品功能和想法，预计这些功能和想法将成为未来TechCrunch活动的固定内容。
欣森说：“我认为我们永远不会放弃我们添加的数字层。”“当我们弄清楚这一切的时候，我们已经体验到了这一层数据和效率，我认为我们未来不能放弃这一层数据和效率。”
在3月下旬，TechCrunch的业务团队决定Disrupt必须是虚拟的，之后，他们开始利用网站编辑格雷格·昆帕拉克提供的笔记和研究，与虚拟活动空间的供应商进行接触。保持内容和演示的质量，最大限度地增加网络机会，尽可能多地保留世博会大厅的感觉，这些都是当务之急。
多年来对初创企业世界的报道起到了帮助作用。Hinson说：“我们基本上跳过了与许多不同公司的合作。”他指出，它最终选择的一些供应商在过去几年里提出或参加了Disrupt。
TechCrunch发现的一些解决方案是后勤方面的，而不是技术上的。例如，为了在不减少会议或演示的情况下缓解屏幕疲劳，TechCrunch将Disrupt从三天扩展到五天，曲目跨越多个时区；美国的Disrupt节目从太平洋时间上午9点开始，通常持续到下午早些时候，而Disrupt Asia节目从太平洋时间晚上10点开始。
但有些解决方案是技术性的。为了为Disrupt的节目创造更吸引人的视觉环境，TechCrunch使用绿色屏幕、真实布景和来自客人的视频电话相结合的方式创建了莫斯科内中心的3D渲染。
TechCrunch执行编辑乔丹·克鲁克(Jordan Crook)表示，Disrupt的节目也充斥着提醒人们，Disrupt正在庆祝其成立十周年。一群曾参加过前几次颠覆活动并发表演讲的演讲者被称为TechCrunch Ten，他们在整个节目中使用了推荐性视频以及一个名为Pitchdeck Teardown的内容片段，其中TechCrunch Ten(包括艾琳·李(Aileen Lee)和德鲁·休斯顿(Drew Houston)等风险资本家)提供了对几家早期初创公司的推介平台的反馈。“TechCrunch Ten”包括艾琳·李(Aileen Lee)和德鲁·休斯顿(Drew Houston)等风险投资者。
克鲁克还在每天的节目中加入了更多的分组讨论和网络会议，活动团队还增加了产品功能，将有相似兴趣的与会者联系起来。一个名为CrunchMatch的网站根据参与者的兴趣向他们推荐参与者，并邀请他们联系；另一个网站会将注册视频聊天时选择相同主题的人随机配对。与会者还可以召集小团体，在编程期间通过视频聊天闲逛。
为了保留在会议大厅随机找到一个展位的偶然性，参展商可以通过通知宣布，他们将在虚拟展位现场展示。
TechCrunch今年将Disrupt的门票和展览价格下调了大约一半：个人门票的起价为350美元，低于去年的695美元，而展览通票的价格为445美元，低于去年的1000美元以上。TechCrunch还以45美元的价格出售Disrupt Digital Pass，它可以进入一个阶段的节目，但不包括CrunchMatch。
Hinson表示，即使按照虚拟活动的标准，这样的价格也是僵硬的，TechCrunch预计今年的出席人数在10,000到15,000人之间，与去年的出席人数大致相同。
欣森说，虽然门票收入将下降，但赞助收入(至少占Disrupt收入的50%)将同比增长，这要归功于更昂贵的赞助套餐；Disrupt的35家赞助商，从AppsFlyer到丰田，今年为套餐支付的费用比去年高出6%。
这在一定程度上是因为数字版的Disrupt提供了面对面活动无法提供的机会。例如，Disrupt将比过去几年提供更多的品牌内容会议和演示，赞助商将能够更容易地追踪哪些人参加了这些演示，让他们有机会收集线索。
Hinson说：“有很多方式来推动注册，这是你在面对面的活动中做不到的。”
虽然大多数媒体公司都在努力解决面对面活动和数字活动之间的收入差异，但许多公司在跟踪和衡量数字环境中发生的事情的能力中看到了机会。体验机构Makeout的联合创始人埃里克·弗莱明(Eric Fleming)说，“我认为，这些系统产生的更多数据的副产品创造了(更有效地)货币化的机会，”体验机构Makeout的联合创始人埃里克·弗莱明(Eric Fleming)说。
但是，弗莱明补充说，许多广告商在承诺支出之前，需要看到这一点奏效的证据。“你需要一个案例研究，才能为广告商提供坚实的论据。”

Help us make your experience with Digiday even better by  taking a quick survey. Respondents will be entered to win a $25 Amazon gift card.
 In a normal year, TechCrunch’s Disrupt conference fits an expo hall, a startup competition, hours of educational and newsmaking content and many days of networking into three days at the Moscone Center in San Francisco.
 Next week, for the event’s tenth anniversary, TechCrunch will try to cram all those things into attendees’ computers, roughly six months after the tech news site’s leadership concluded that an in-person version of the event would be impossible.
 Disrupt 2020 will feature approximately the same number of attendees — at least 10,000 — and about the same number of expo presenters — 485 — that its 2019 event drew, said TechCrunch director of operations Joey Hinson.
 But it has also hit upon a number of new product features and ideas that it expects will become fixtures in future TechCrunch events.
 “I don’t think we’re ever going to walk away from the digital layer we’ve added,” Hinson said. “There’s this layer of data and efficiency that we’ve experienced as we’ve figured this all out that I don’t think we can walk away from in the future.”
 After deciding in late March that Disrupt would have to be virtual, TechCrunch’s business team began reaching out to vendors in the virtual event space, using notes and research provided by one of the site’s editors, Greg Kumparak. Preserving the quality of the content and presentations, maximizing networking opportunities and preserving as much of the feel of the expo hall as possible were among the top priorities.
 Years of covering the startup world helped. “We basically skipped the line with a lot of different companies,” Hinson said, noting that some of the vendors it wound up choosing had presented or attended Disrupt in years past.
 Some solutions TechCrunch hit upon were logistical rather than technical. To mitigate screen fatigue without cutting down on sessions or presentations, for example, TechCrunch expanded Disrupt from three days to five, with tracks running across multiple time zones; U.S. Disrupt programming begins at 9am, PT, and normally runs until early afternoon, while Disrupt Asia programming begins after 10pm, PT.
 But some solutions were technological. To create a more visually engaging setting for Disrupt’s programming, TechCrunch created a 3D rendering of the Moscone Center using a combination of green screens, real sets and video call-ins from guests.
 Disrupt’s programming is also crammed with reminders that Disrupt is celebrating its ten year anniversary, TechCrunch managing editor Jordan Crook said. A group of presenters who have attended and presented at previous Disrupts, called the TechCrunch Ten, are layered throughout the programming, using testimonial videos as well as a content segment called the Pitchdeck Teardown, in which the TechCrunch Ten, which includes venture capitalists such as Aileen Lee and Drew Houston, offer feedback on several early stage startups’ pitch decks.
 Crook built more breakout and networking sessions into each day’s programming too, and the events team added product features to connect attendees with similar interests. One, called CrunchMatch, suggests attendees to one another based on their interests and invites them to connect; another will randomly pair people who selected the same topics when they registered for video chats. Attendees can also convene small groups and hang out via video chat during programming sessions.
 To preserve the serendipity of finding a random booth in the convention hall, exhibitors can announce, using notifications, that they are hanging out live at their virtual booths.
 TechCrunch cut ticket and exhibition prices for Disrupt this year roughly in half: Prices for individual tickets begin at $350, down from $695 last year, while exhibition passes cost $445, down from over $1,000 last year. TechCrunch is also selling a Disrupt Digital Pass for $45 that offers access to one stage of programming, but does not include CrunchMatch.
 Even at those prices, stiff by virtual event standards, Hinson said TechCrunch expects between 10,000 and 15,000 attendees this year, roughly in line with last year’s attendance figures.
  While ticket revenue will be down, sponsorship revenue, which accounts for at least 50% of Disrupt’s revenues, will be up year over year, thanks to more expensive sponsorship packages; Disrupt’s 35 sponsors, which range from AppsFlyer to Toyota, are paying 6% more for packages this year than last year, Hinson said.
 That’s partly because the digital version of Disrupt offers opportunities that an in-person event wouldn’t. For example, Disrupt will feature more branded content sessions and presentations than in years past, and sponsors will be able to more easily track who attends those presentations, giving them a chance to gather leads.
 “There are a lot of ways to drive registrations in a way that you can’t do at an in-person event,” Hinson said.
 While most media companies are grappling with the differences in revenue between in-person and digital events, many see opportunities in the ability to track and measure what happens in a digital environment. “I think the byproduct of there being a lot more data that gets generated as a result of these systems creates that opportunity [to monetize more effectively],” said Eric Fleming, the cofounder of the experiential agency Makeout.
 But, Fleming added, many advertisers will need to see proof that that works before committing to spending. “You kind of need a case study to have a solid argument for an advertiser.”
title: TechCrunch如何移动到Disrupt Online，扩展到5天以缓解屏幕疲劳，并使用绿色屏幕和套装创建Moscon Center的3D渲染
title_en: How TechCrunch moved Disrupt online, expanding to 5 days to mitigate screen fatigue and creating a 3D rendering of Moscone Center using green screens and sets

note:
爱德华·斯诺登(Edward Snowden)丑闻曝光时，美国国家安全局(NSA)一名前局长正在担任该职位，他已加入亚马逊董事会，担任董事。
基思·亚历山大将军的任命于周三公之于众，他是在斯诺登揭发中情局大规模监控项目后为公众所知的。
亚历山大将军的任命公布数小时后，斯诺登在Twitter上表达了他的担忧。
是的，基思·亚历山大亲自对引发全球丑闻的非法大规模监控项目负责，他补充道。
上周，美国一家联邦法院裁定，斯诺登在2013年发现的美国国家安全局(NSA)的电话记录监听活动是非法的。
亚历山大将军当时建议，记者不应该被允许报道斯诺登泄露的文件。
2013年，他告诉The Verge：“我认为，那些报社记者拥有所有这些文件，5万份-无论他们拥有什么，都在出售，就好像这些-你知道这是没有意义的，这是错误的。”
隐私国际(Privacy International)的倡导总监埃丁·奥马诺维奇(Edin Omanovic)表示，亚马逊聘用亚历山大将军的决定令人深感担忧。
他说，每天，像亚马逊这样的大型科技公司都会在没有任何民主问责的情况下做出影响人们生活的重大道德决定。
显然，令人担忧的是，现在处于这一问题核心的是一个花了数年时间为秘密数据窃取辩护的人，这些窃取后来被证明是非法的。我们不需要另一个国家安全局--即使是有隐私政策的国家安全局。
奥马诺维奇暗示，此举可能使亚马逊更容易在安全市场发挥影响力。
亚马逊现在想要挤进利润丰厚的国防和安全市场，并借此获得政府拥有的庞大数据存储的钥匙。
要做到这一点，关键是聘用有影响力的前公务员，这已经不是什么秘密了。他补充说，由此产生的权力集中不利于个人、不利于公司、也不利于社会。
去年，有消息称亚马逊的Alexa是各种智能语音助手中的一员，这些语音助手一直在将语音录音发送给人类承包商进行审查。
note_en:
A former National Security Agency (NSA) chief who was in the post when the Edward Snowden scandal broke has joined Amazon's board as a director.
 Gen Keith Alexander, whose appointment was made public on Wednesday,  became known to the public after Mr Snowden blew the whistle on the agency's mass surveillance programs.
    Hours after Gen Alexander's appointment was made public Mr Snowden took to Twitter to voice his concern.
  "Yes, the Keith Alexander personally responsible for the unlawful mass surveillance programs that caused a global scandal," he added.
 Last week, a US federal court ruled that the NSA's phone record surveillance, uncovered by Mr Snowden in 2013, was illegal.
 Gen Alexander suggested at the time that reporters should not have been allowed to report on the documents that were leaked by Mr Snowden.
 “I think it’s wrong that that newspaper reporters have all these documents, the 50,000-whatever they have and are selling them and giving them out as if these - you know it just doesn’t make sense,” he told the Verge in 2013.
   Edin Omanovic, advocacy director at Privacy International, said Amazon's decision to hire Gen Alexander was deeply concerning.
 "Every day, big tech companies like Amazon make huge ethical decisions which affect people’s lives without any democratic accountability," he said.
 "Clearly it is worrying that at the heart of this will now be someone who spent years defending secret data grabs which were later proved to be unlawful. We don’t need another NSA - even one with a privacy policy."
 Mr Omanovic suggested the move could make it easier for Amazon to exercise its influence in the security market.
 "Amazon now wants to corner in on the lucrative defence and security market - and with that the keys to the huge data stores held by governments.
 "It is no secret that the key to this is hiring influential former public servants. The concentration of power that will arise from this is bad for people, bad for companies, and bad for societies," he added.
 Last year, it emerged that Amazon's Alexa was among a variety of smart voice assistants  that had been sending voice recordings to human contractors for review.
title: 斯诺登批评亚马逊聘用美国国家安全局前老板
title_en: Snowden criticises Amazon for hiring former NSA boss

欢迎来到Ars Cardboard，我们的周末看桌面游戏！请访问cardboard.arstechnica.com查看我们完整的棋盘游戏报道。
自2009年首次发布以来，竞争异常激烈的家庭战略游戏“小世界”(Small World)已成为常年的桌面宠儿。具有讽刺意味的是，考虑到它的名字，它的成功导致它的幻想领域多年来不断增长，源源不断的扩张引入了新的派别来指挥，新的地区来探索。不过现在，这款游戏已经转移到了一个全新的背景，小魔兽世界，一款以魔兽人出没的艾泽拉斯为背景的独立游戏。
对于外行来说，最初的小世界游戏让玩家控制着敌对的派系，所有人都在争先恐后地夺取领土，扩大自己的帝国。虽然这是无数战略游戏玩家的共同前提，但小世界对这一概念的不可预测的看法伴随着一些曲折，帮助它脱颖而出。
最大的转折是，与许多游戏几个世纪以来让玩家掌控单一文明不同，小世界让事情变得不那么持久。它的帝国起起落落，就像现实世界一样。在游戏开始时，你将从精灵、兽人、高耸的巨人和野蛮的鼠人中选择一个种族来指挥。每一种都有自己的特殊能力，在移动、得分、进攻或防守区域时给你带来优势。每经过一次，你的部队就会在游戏的地图上蔓延得更远，但不可避免地会过度扩张你的边界，让你的文明走向衰落，迫使你选择一个新的文明来从头开始这个过程。
不过，你选择的种族只是方程式的一半。每当你选择一个派别时，你还将获得一个从随机堆栈中获得的相关特殊异能令牌。这意味着你将在每次试玩中从不同的力量组合中进行选择，这使得“小世界”成为一场变化无常的形势和变化无常的战术的游戏。
这款游戏将其久经考验的模式巧妙地带入了这个新的背景，派系直接来自魔兽世界的传说。每个人都感觉自己在电脑游戏中的表现是真实的。矮人在入侵山区时获得力量提升，爬行洞穴的狗头人可以穿越地下地道网络，精通技术的侏儒可以用飞行器攻击遥远的地区，居住在海洋中的纳加人可以占据对手无法进入的湖泊和海域。不同的种族也与部落和联盟(争夺艾泽拉斯控制权的强大交战团体)结盟。当你可以控制任何派系的派系时，只要你现在的部队在战斗中击败他们的死敌，你就会获得加分。
另一个与最初的小世界蓝图大不相同的地方在于游戏的地图。“小魔兽世界”将战场划分为一系列岛屿，每个岛屿都有自己的迷你板，而不是为一块陆地而战。这是一个方便的方式来扩大或缩小游戏以适应不同的玩家数量，而且它还会抛出一些关于你想要将有限的部队投入到哪里的决定。
一些地区还提供了代表魔法物品和重要地点的代币，每个代币都赋予在任何给定时间持有它的人一个新的能力-战斗中的增益，提高你的得分，或者增加投入战斗的部队。这是直接取自2011年的“小世界：地下世界”中的一个元素，这意味着地图上这些令人垂涎的地点成为每个人扩张野心的目标，在游戏过程中反复易手，迫使你思考你愿意向他们投掷多少军队。
这一切都使得这款游戏比经典的小世界更有深度-但这里没有改变，这是一种改进。
一个问题是，游戏中的一些特殊能力只有在特定条件下才有效。例如，兽人是部落的成员，每当他们像人类或矮人一样成功地攻击联盟种族时，他们就会获得加分。但是，由于联盟、部落和中立种族的混合可供玩家指挥，有时你根本就没有一个有效的攻击目标。
这在双人游戏中是一个特别的问题。而且，随着桌上玩家的减少，任何时候出现在棋盘上的可用的比赛也更少了，这一事实让情况变得更加复杂。这剥夺了一些来自特殊力量之间互动的多样性和深度。
因此，很难推荐将“小魔兽世界”作为双人游戏。只有在竞争对手更多的情况下，它才能发挥作用，特别是当你有足够的玩家来尝试其基于团队的艾泽拉斯之战变体时，它确保了战斗中总是有联盟和部落种族，并看到合作伙伴试图提出联合战略。虽然可以尝试玩家人数较少的游戏，但游戏建议使用四到五个玩家。
这是一部老牌热门电影的最新衍生品，值得你花钱购买吗？答案是：这要视情况而定。如果你尝试过“小世界”，但并不喜欢，这里没有什么能神奇地改变你的想法。如果你是原始游戏的粉丝，这是一个稍微更深层次的版本，尽管它回收了以前版本中的一些想法，同时引入了其他使其与2009年游戏不兼容的想法。如果你是小世界的新手，随着时间的推移，原始游戏的扩展集合提供了更多的发现，以及更多的方法来调整它的设置。
那么，最喜欢小魔兽世界的人，就是那些在最喜欢的环境中寻找一款可访问的、基于团队的战略游戏的魔兽世界粉丝。这就是为什么团队游戏，它修复了个人设置的许多问题，被包括在一个可选的附加变量中，感觉很奇怪。一旦你试过了，这是你唯一想玩的方式。
note_en:
Welcome to Ars Cardboard, our weekend look at tabletop games! Check out our complete board gaming coverage at  cardboard.arstechnica.com.
  Since it was first published in 2009, the hyper-competitive family strategy game  Small World has become a perennial tabletop favorite. Ironically, given its name, its success has caused its fantasy realm to grow over the years, with a steady stream of expansions introducing new factions to command and new regions to explore. Now, though, the game has shifted to an entirely new setting with  Small World of Warcraft, a standalone game set in WoW's orc-infested land of Azeroth.
 For the uninitiated, the original  Small World has players control rival factions, all competing to seize territory and expand their empires. While it’s a premise shared by countless "dudes-on-a-map" strategy games,  Small World's unpredictable take on the concept comes with a handful of twists that help it stand out from the crowd.
  The biggest twist is that, unlike many games which put players in command of a single civilization over centuries,  Small World makes things far less permanent. Its empires rise and fall just like in the real world. At the beginning of the game, you’ll choose a race to command from a selection including elves, orcs, towering giants, and savage rat-men. Each comes with its own special ability, giving you advantages when it comes to movement, scoring, attacking, or defending regions. With each passing round, you’ll spread your troops further across the game’s map but inevitably will overextend your borders, sending your civilization into decline and forcing you to choose a new one to start the process from scratch.
 Your chosen race is only one half of the equation, though. Whenever you choose a faction, you’ll also gain an associated special power token taken from a random stack. This means you will select from different combinations of powers on every play-through, which makes  Small World a game of shifting situations and mutable tactics.
  The game carries its tried and tested formula neatly into this new setting with factions drawn straight from  World of Warcraft lore. Each feels true to its presentation in the PC game. Dwarves get a strength boost when invading mountainous regions, cave-crawling kobolds can traverse a network of underground tunnels, tech-savvy gnomes attack distant regions with flying machines, and ocean-dwelling naga can occupy lake and sea spaces inaccessible to their rivals. Different races also come aligned to the Horde and the Alliance (the powerful warring groups who battle for control of Azeroth). And while you can control factions of any affiliation, you’ll gain bonus points whenever your current troops defeat their sworn enemies in battle.
 The other big departure from the original  Small World blueprint lies in the game’s map. Rather than fighting over a single land mass,  Small World of Warcraft divides the battlefield into a chain of islands, each represented by its own mini board. It’s a handy way to grow or shrink the game to accommodate different player counts, and it also throws up some decisions about exactly where you want to commit your limited forces.
 Some regions also come with tokens representing magic items and important locations, each of which grants a new ability to whomever holds it at any given time—buffs in battle, boosts to your score, or extra troops to throw into the fight. It’s an element taken directly from 2011’s  Small World: Underground, and it means that these coveted spots on the map become the target of everyone’s expansionist ambitions, switching hands repeatedly during the course of the game and forcing you to think about how much of your army you’re willing to throw at them.
 It all makes for a game with a little more depth than classic  Small World—but not change here is an improvement.
 One issue is that some of the special powers in the game are only effective under certain conditions. Orcs, for example, are members of the Horde, and they gain bonus points whenever they successfully attack Alliance races like humans or dwarves. But with a mix of Alliance, Horde, and neutral races available for players to command, there are times when you simply won’t have a valid target for your aggression.
 It’s a particular problem in two-player games. And it’s compounded by the fact that with fewer players at the table, fewer of the available races are present on the board at any one time. This strips some of the variety and depth that comes from the interaction between special powers.
  For this reason, it’s hard to recommend  Small World of Warcraft as a two-player game. It only comes into its own with more rivals in the contest, especially when you have enough players to try its team-based "Battle for Azeroth" variant, which ensures there are always Alliance and Horde races in the fight and sees partners attempting to come up with joint strategies. While it’s possible to try this with lower player counts, the game recommends four or five.
 Is this latest spin-off of an established hit worth spending your money on? The answer is: it depends. If you’ve tried  Small World and didn’t enjoy it, there’s nothing here that will magically change your mind. If you’re a fan of the original game, this is a slightly deeper version, albeit one that recycles some ideas from previous releases while introducing others that make it incompatible with the 2009 game. And if you’re a newcomer to  Small World, the original game’s collection of expansions offers more to discover over time and more ways to tweak its setup.
 The folks who will enjoy  Small World of Warcraft most, then, are  World of Warcraft fans looking for an accessible, team-based strategy game in a favorite setting. That's why it feels odd that the team game, which fixes many of the problems with the individual setup, is included as an optional add-on variant. Once you’ve tried that, it’s the only way you’ll ever want to play.

阿斯特拉并不指望在这次飞行中真正到达轨道-它一直表示，它的目标是在火箭的三次试飞内到达轨道，在这次第一次任务之前，它表示，主要目标是在这次飞行中有一个很好的第一阶段燃烧。当然，这并不是名义上的第一阶段燃烧，因为那是故障发生的时候，但该公司在一篇博客文章中仍然指出，根据他们对数据的第一次审查，“火箭表现非常好”。
阿斯特拉说，任务提前结束是因为火箭在上升过程中似乎出现了一些不想要的来回摆动，这导致了车辆自动安全系统关闭了发动机。这实际上也是个好消息，因为这意味着阿斯特拉为确保安全故障也能按设计工作而采取的步骤。你可以在上面的视频中看到，火箭发动机的灯在飞行过程中简单地熄灭了，然后一段时间后，它撞击地面产生了一个火球。
值得注意的是，大多数全新火箭的首次飞行都没有完全按计划进行-包括SpaceX的首次飞行，该公司的创始人兼首席执行官埃隆·马斯克在推特上表达了对阿斯特拉团队的鼓励。同样，火箭实验室的彼得·贝克也表示支持。更不用说阿斯特拉一直在极端条件下运营，由于新冠肺炎危机，在阿拉斯加只有6人的地面团队部署了不到一周就设置好的发射系统。
阿斯特拉肯定能够从这次发射中获得许多有价值的数据，这些数据可以用来提高下一次尝试顺利进行的机会。该公司指出，它预计将在“未来几周”审查上述数据，因为它正在进行这一系列三次尝试中的第二次飞行。火箭3.2，那次任务的测试物品，已经完成，等待着那次尝试。
note_en:
Astra wasn’t expecting to actually reach orbit on this particular flight – it has always said that its goal is to reach orbit within three test flights of Rocket, and prior to this first mission, said that the main goal was to have a good first-stage burn on this one specifically. This wasn’t a nominal first-stage burn, of course, since that’s when the failure occurred, but the company still noted in a  blog post that “the rocket performed very well” according to their first reviews of the data.
  The mission ended early because of what appears to be a bit of unwanted back-and-forth wobbling in the rocket as it ascended, Astra said, which caused an engine shutdown by the vehicle’s automated safety system. That’s actually also good news, since it means the steps Astra has taken to ensure safe failures are also working as designed. You can see in the video above that the light of the rocket’s engines simply go out during flight, and then some time later there’s a fireball from its impact on the ground.
 It’s worth noting that most first flights of entirely new rockets don’t go entirely as planned – including those by  SpaceX,   whose founder and  CEO Elon Musk expressed his encouragement to the Astra team on Twitter. Likewise,  Rocket Lab’s Peter Beck also chimed in with support. Not to mention that Astra has been operating under extreme conditions, with just a six-person team on the ground in Alaska to deploy the launch system, which was set up in under a week, due to the COVID-19 crisis.
 Astra will definitely be able to get a lot of valuable data out of this launch that it can use to put towards improving the chances of its next try going well. The company notes that it expects to review said data “over the next several weeks” as it proceeds towards the second flight in this series of three attempts. Rocket 3.2, the test article for that mission, is already completed and awaiting that try.

“你有没有看到条纹的首席营销官出局了？”一天，一位创始人在我身后的办公桌上问道。
虽然我听说了这个消息，但这个问题让我很好奇，原因有两个：首先，我刚刚担任金融科技(音译)一家快速增长的初创公司的营销主管两个月，很想知道我的老板对这个话题的看法。其次，我密切关注了这位有问题的女性，并认为她是一位出色的营销者。
“才过去一年，”他继续说。“她一定很坏。”
几年后，这种交流让我记忆犹新，因为它象征着一些技术创始人对营销角色的看法。
这个想法是这样的：创始人创造伟大的产品，通过网络效应的力量来销售自己。当未来出现需要时，公司会聘请一位经验丰富的营销专业人士，在每个人都在全力冲刺的情况下，从头开始创建一个部门。如果不成功，…。她一定很坏。
事实上，CMO的短暂任期和突然离职已经成为硅谷的常态。据我统计，罗宾汉的前三位CMO的任期加起来是两年；Wealthfront的第一位CMO持续了一年半；Intercom和Acfirm的第一位CMO都在不到一年的时间里就推出了。在Dropbox，我们的第一位首席营销官(我发现他是一位出色的领导者和营销者)在Dropbox工作了一年多一点。…。
这些营销人员并不都是“坏”的。事实上，他们中的大多数都享有极高的声誉。那么到底是怎么回事呢？
创始人往往会尽可能长时间地忽视营销，并说服自己这是“空洞的”。产品经理可能会处理客户调查、产品定位和产品发布的外部组件等事务，然后将它们交给销售人员，对吗？毕竟，他拥有MBA学位。：)
这在早期阶段效果很好，但随后出现了问题：面向客户的团队成员难以在市场上坚持一致的信息；信息在客户和产品团队之间没有流动；轮流管理公司的Twitter账户不起作用；创始人必须管理媒体别名。
与此同时，市场营销的内部需求也在增长：工程需要更好的市场洞察力；产品需要成功发布；销售需要更有说服力的材料；高管需要外部沟通支持；人力资源需要招聘和留住帮助；客户成功需要扩展和自动化大量客户沟通。
最后，有些事情发生了。公司陷入公关危机，需要向新市场扩张，或者面临新的竞争。如果公司有更好的品牌资产、知名度或公众情绪，高管们就能专注于重要的事情。
在这一点上，创始人“屈服”，并开始寻找营销主管来构建功能。
在招聘第一位营销主管时，创始人有时会热情洋溢地宣称：“看看我们已经走了多远--而且没有花一分钱在营销上！”
我告诉他们，这就好比走进牙医的办公室，宣称，“看看我的牙齿看起来多棒--而且一天都不刷牙！”你想成为我的第一位牙医吗？“。讨厌。
如果你多年来一直不相信市场营销，那么你很可能从一开始就没有为这个功能的成功做好准备。有经验的营销人员知道这一点。
如果他们以前做过这项工作，应聘者会知道他们会同时做四份工作：
工作2：定义营销对公司意味着什么，并教育团队其他成员；
他们也知道，他们将陷入一片混乱，并对期望快速见效的高管负责。此外，在没有团队的情况下，他们将负责他们多年来委派的日常活动(撰写文案、设置营销仪表盘等)。
与其遵循这个失败的策略，我建议初创公司在这个过程中更早地考虑营销，并开始为未来的领导者奠定基础。
成立一个由一到两个强大的个人贡献者组成的小团队来承担营销职责。这些人应该是能够处理各种营销任务的多面手(也许可以考虑让他们成为产品营销经理)。让他们为您的网站和博客设置基本的基础设施，如连接到您的CRM和Google Analytics的营销自动化工具。将他们连接到其他部门，开始构建针对转换进行优化的网站、开发简单的活动、起草品牌信息等。
这对创始人来说是一个胜利，他们在这个过程中很早就学会了不同的营销职能。对于商业多面手来说，这是一个胜利，他们可以在对公司产生影响的同时找到自己的激情。这对未来的营销主管来说是一个胜利，因为有人已经做了一些试错，并使营销职能开始发挥作用。这对招聘者来说是一场胜利，因为团队中已经有人在工作，这份工作更有吸引力。
这些超级IC不会做营销主管的所有工作。他们不会招募，甚至不一定会领导。但你会有懂市场营销、会说英语、能做实验的人。
就像NFL教练一样，营销主管经历了大量的更替。由于营销计划通常很难衡量，而且角色也不是面向客户的，因此取消营销主管是创始人在事情不顺利的第一个迹象时迅速而容易地拉动的杠杆。
因此，一种自我实现的预言产生了，营销人员认为他们只会持续一年，并开始基于这一信念做出决定。知道时间有限，他们会变得过于雄心勃勃，行动太快，或者专注于能充实简历的事情，而不是战略优先事项。一两年后，他们去了另一家公司，这家初创公司又回到了起点。
这对初创企业来说是不好的！由于与第一位营销主管离职的经验有关，许多创始人推迟了一段时间招聘新的主管-有时会让公司的情况比营销主管加入之前更糟糕。
为了避免你的公司遭遇这种命运，请使用以下核对表来确定你是否准备好担任营销主管：
您是否有预算、员工人数和招聘带宽来帮助新的营销领导者建立他们的团队？
您是否清楚地了解您的增长模式，以及营销努力在哪些方面能够对增长产生独特的影响？
您是否有来自工程和设计领导层的支持来支持市场试验和活动？
你有耐心在大获全胜之前留出时间进行试验和一些失败吗？
如果你对这些问题(大部分)的回答是“是”，那么恭喜你--你已经准备好聘用你的第一个营销主管了。祝好运!
note_en:
“Did you see that Stripe’s CMO is out?” a founder asked one day from the desk behind me.
 Though I’d heard the news, the question had me curious for two reasons: First, I was two months into my own role as head of marketing of a fast-growing fintech startup and curious what my boss thought about the topic. Second, I had closely followed the woman in question and considered her a terrific marketer.
 “It’s only been a year,” he continued. “She must’ve been bad.”
  This exchange sticks with me years later because it’s emblematic of the mentality some technical founders hold about the role of marketing.
 The thinking goes: Founders build great products that sell themselves through the power of network effects. When the need arises down the road, the company will hire a proven marketing professional to start a department from scratch while everyone’s at a full sprint. If it doesn’t work out… she must’ve been bad.
 In fact, brief tenures and abrupt departures of CMOs have become the norm in Silicon Valley.  By my count, the tenures of Robinhood’s first  three CMOs combined add up to two years; Wealthfront’s first CMO lasted a year and half; and Intercom’s and Affirm’s first CMOs were both out in less than a year. At Dropbox, our first CMO (who I found to be a fantastic leader and marketer) stayed on for just over a year…
 These marketers weren’t all “bad.” Most of them have stellar reputations, in fact. So what’s really going on?
    Founders often disregard marketing for as long as possible, convincing themselves it’s “fluff.” A product manager can probably handle things like customer research, product positioning, and the external components of a product launch, and then hand them off to sales, right? After all, he has an MBA. :)
 This works well enough in the early stages, but then problems emerge: Customer-facing team members struggle to stick to a consistent message in the market; information isn’t flowing between customers and product teams; taking turns managing the company Twitter account isn’t working; and the founders have to manage the press alias.
 At the same time, the internal demand for marketing grows: Engineering needs better market insights; product needs successful launches; sales needs more compelling materials; executives need external comms support; HR needs help with recruiting and retention; and customer success needs to scale and automate a lot of customer comms.
 Finally, something happens. The company gets ensnared in a PR crisis, needs to expand to new markets, or faces new competition. If only the company had better brand equity, visibility, or public sentiment, executives could stay focused on what’s important.
 At this point, founders “give in” and kick off the search for a head of marketing to build the function.
   When recruiting for their first head of marketing, founders will sometimes declare enthusiastically, “Look how far we’ve gotten—and without a penny spent on marketing!”
 I tell them this is analogous to walking into a dentist’s office and proclaiming, “Look how great my teeth look—and without a day of brushing! Do you want to be my first dentist?” Yuck.
 If you didn’t believe in marketing for years, you probably aren’t set up for the function to succeed in the first place. Experienced marketers know this.
 If they’ve done the job before, a candidate will know they’ll be juggling four jobs at the same time:
 Job 2: Define what marketing means at the company and educate the rest of the team;
 They also know they’ll be stepping into a mess and answering to executives who expect quick results. What’s more, with no team in place, they’ll be responsible for day-to-day activities they’ve been delegating for years (writing copy, setting up marketing dashboards, etc.)
    Instead of following this failed playbook, I advise startups to think about marketing much earlier in the process and begin laying the foundation that a future leader can build on.
 Set up a small team of one or two strong individual contributors to take on marketing duties. These should be generalists capable of handling a variety of marketing tasks (maybe consider making them  product marketing managers). Have them set up basic infrastructure like a marketing automation tool that connects to your CRM and Google Analytics for your website and blog. Connect them to other departments to begin building websites optimized for conversions, developing simple campaigns, drafting brand messaging, and so on.
 This is a win for the founder, who learns the different marketing functions much earlier in the process. It’s a win for the business generalist, who can find their passion while making an impact at the company. It’s a win for the future head of marketing, because someone will have already done some trial-and-error and gotten the marketing function off the ground. And it’s a win for the recruiter, because the job’s more attractive with someone already on the team cranking.
 These super ICs won’t do all the jobs of a marketing head. They won’t recruit or necessarily even lead. But you’ll have people who understand marketing, speak the language, and can run experiments.
   Like NFL coaches, heads of marketing experience a lot of turnover. Because marketing initiatives are often hard to measure and roles aren’t customer-facing, removing a marketing lead is a quick and easy lever for a founder to pull at the first signs of things not working out.
 As a result, a self-fulfilling prophecy develops, where marketers  assume they’ll only last a year and start making decisions based on that belief. Knowing time is limited, they get over-ambitious and move too fast or focus on things that will pad their resume rather than strategic priorities. After a year or two, they’re off to another company and the startup is back at square one.
 This is bad for startups! Scarred by their experience dealing with a first head of marketing departure, many founders put off hiring a new lead for a while—sometimes leaving the company even worse off than before the head of marketing joined.
   To avoid this fate for your company, use the following checklist to determine if you’re ready for a marketing head:
 Do you have budget, headcount, and recruiting bandwidth to help a new marketing leader build their team?
 Do you have a clear understanding of your growth model and where marketing efforts can uniquely impact growth?
 Do you have buy-in from engineering and design leadership to support marketing experiments and campaigns?
 Do you have the patience to allow time for experimentation and some fails before big wins?
 If you answer “yes” to (most of) these questions, congratulations—you’re ready to hire your first marketing head. Good luck!
